claims.flip_evidence_text: any amendment to any material term of such Superior Proposal shall require a new Notice of Recommendation Change and a new Notice Period, except that the four (4) Business Day Notice Period referred to in clause (D) above shall instead be equal to the longer of (1) three (3) Business Days or (2) the period remaining under the Notice Period under clause (D) above immediately prior to the delivery of such additional notice under this clause (D)
claims.notes: The claim incorrectly attributes discretion to the Board by stating it 'may require' a new notice. The contract provides that any material amendment automatically 'shall require' a new Notice of Recommendation Change and a new Notice Period; the requirement is mandatory, not permissive.

prompt:
You are a proxy legal annotator for a research pilot. Use only the contract excerpt, the question, the model answer, and the fixed claim list.

For each claim return:
- correctness_label: true, false, or partially_true
- load_bearing_label: yes or no
- flip_evidence_text: required if load_bearing_label=yes, else empty string
- notes: optional short note

Contract excerpt:
Section 5.4 Acquisition Proposals. <omitted> (b) <omitted> (iv) Notwithstanding anything in this Agreement to the contrary, with respect to an Acquisition Proposal, the Board of Directors of VEREIT or Board of Directors of Realty Income, as applicable, may make a Change in VEREIT Recommendation or a Change in Realty Income Recommendation, as applicable (and in the event that the Board of Directors of VEREIT determines such Acquisition Proposal to be a Superior Proposal, in accordance with this Section 5.4, terminate this Agreement pursuant to Section 7.1(d)(i)), in each case (including with respect to any such termination), if and only if (A) an unsolicited bona fide written Acquisition Proposal (that did not result from a material breach of this Section 5.4) is made to VEREIT or Realty Income, as applicable, by a third party, and such Acquisition Proposal is not withdrawn, (B) the Board of Directors of VEREIT or the Board of Directors of Realty Income, as applicable, has concluded in good faith (after consultation with outside legal counsel and financial advisors) that such Acquisition Proposal constitutes a Superior Proposal, (C) the Board of Directors of VEREIT or of Realty Income, as applicable, has concluded in good faith (after consultation with its outside legal counsel) that failure to do so would reasonably be expected to result in a breach of its duties to VEREIT or Realty Income, as applicable, (D) four (4) Business Days (the “Notice Period”) shall have elapsed since the party proposing to take such action has given written notice to the other party advising such other party that the notifying party intends to take such action and specifying in reasonable detail the reasons therefor, including the terms and conditions of any such Superior Proposal that is the basis of the proposed action (a “Notice of Recommendation Change”) (it being understood that any amendment to any material term of such Superior Proposal shall require a new Notice of Recommendation Change and a new Notice Period, except that the four (4) Business Day Notice Period referred to in clause (D) above shall instead be equal to the longer of (1) three (3) Business Days or (2) the period remaining under the Notice Period under clause (D) above immediately prior to the delivery of such additional notice under this clause (D)), (E) during the Notice Period, the notifying party has considered and, at the reasonable request of the other party, engaged in good faith discussions with such party regarding, any adjustment or modification of the terms of this Agreement proposed by the other party, and (F) the Board of Directors of the party proposing to take such action, following the Notice Period, again reasonably determines in good faith (after consultation with outside legal counsel, and taking into account any adjustment or modification of the terms of this Agreement proposed by the other party) that failure to do so would reasonably be expected to result in a breach of its duties to such party. Superior Proposal, in accordance with this Section 5.4, terminate this Agreement pursuant to Section 7.1(d)(i)), in each case (including with respect to any such termination), if and only if (A) an unsolicited bona fide written Acquisition Proposal (that did not result from a material breach of this Section 5.4) is made to VEREIT or Realty Income, as applicable, by a third party, and such Acquisition Proposal is not withdrawn, (B) the Board of Directors of VEREIT or the Board of Directors of Realty Income, as applicable, has concluded in good faith (after consultation with outside legal counsel and financial advisors) that such Acquisition Proposal constitutes a Superior Proposal, (C) the Board of Directors of VEREIT or of Realty Income, as applicable, has concluded in good faith (after consultation with its outside legal counsel) that failure to do so would reasonably be expected to result in a breach of its duties to VEREIT or Realty Income, as applicable, (D) four (4) intends to take such action and specifying in reasonable detail the reasons therefor, including the terms and conditions of any such Superior Proposal that is the basis of the proposed action (a “Notice of Recommendation Change”) (it being understood that any amendment to any material term of such Superior Proposal shall require a new Notice of Recommendation Change and a new Notice Period, (Page 30) Section 5.4 Acquisition Proposals. <omitted> (b) <omitted> (iv) <omitted> the Board of Directors of VEREIT <omitted> may make a Change in VEREIT Recommendation <omitted> if and only if <omitted> (D) four (4) Business Days (the “Notice Period”) shall have elapsed since the party proposing to take such action has given written notice to the other party advising such other party that the notifying party intends to take such action <omitted> (it being understood that any amendment to any material term of such Superior Proposal shall require a new Notice of Recommendation Change and a new Notice Period, except that the four (4) Business Day Notice Period referred to in clause (D) above shall instead be equal to the longer of (1) three (3) Business Days or (2) the period remaining under the Notice Period under clause (D) above immediately prior to the delivery of such additional notice under this clause (D)) (Page 30)

Question:
Number of additional matching rights periods for modifications (COR)

Llama answer:
The Board of Directors of VEREIT or Realty Income, as applicable, may require a new Notice of Recommendation Change and a new Notice Period for any amendment to any material term of a Superior Proposal, except that the four (4) Business Day Notice Period shall instead be equal to the longer of (1) three (3) Business Days or (2) the period remaining under the Notice Period under clause (D) above immediately prior to the delivery of such additional notice.

Canonical claims:
- claim-3d7dfa9bf8de: The Board of Directors of VEREIT or Realty Income, as applicable, may require a new Notice of Recommendation Change and a new Notice Period for any amendment to any material term of a Superior Proposal, except that the four (4) Business Day Notice Period shall instead be equal to the longer of (1) three (3) Business Days or (2) the period remaining under the Notice Period under clause (D) above immediately prior to the delivery of such additional notice.

message.reasoning:
The user wants me to act as a proxy legal annotator. I need to evaluate the single claim provided against the contract excerpt, the question, and the model answer (Llama answer).

First, let's understand the task:
- I have a contract excerpt (Section 5.4 Acquisition Proposals).
- I have a question: "Number of additional matching rights periods for modifications (COR)"
- I have a Llama answer.
- I have a canonical claim list with one claim: claim-3d7dfa9bf8de.

For this claim, I need to return:
- correctness_label: true, false, or partially_true
- load_bearing_label: yes or no
- flip_evidence_text: required if load_bearing_label=yes, else empty string
- notes: optional short note

Let's analyze the claim:
claim-3d7dfa9bf8de: "The Board of Directors of VEREIT or Realty Income, as applicable, may require a new Notice of Recommendation Change and a new Notice Period for any amendment to any material term of a Superior Proposal, except that the four (4) Business Day Notice Period shall instead be equal to the longer of (1) three (3) Business Days or (2) the period remaining under the Notice Period under clause (D) above immediately prior to the delivery of such additional notice."

Now let's check the contract excerpt. The relevant text appears in the first full excerpt and the last excerpt:
"(it being understood that any amendment to any material term of such Superior Proposal shall require a new Notice of Recommendation Change and a new Notice Period, except that the four (4) Business Day Notice Period referred to in clause (D) above shall instead be equal to the longer of (1) three (3) Business Days or (2) the period remaining under the Notice Period under clause (D) above immediately prior to the delivery of such additional notice under this clause (D))"

Wait, the claim says "The Board ... may require...". But the contract says "any amendment to any material term of such Superior Proposal shall require a new Notice...". The claim uses "may require" while the contract uses "shall require". This is a significant difference. "Shall" indicates it is mandatory, not discretionary ("may"). So the claim is partially true or false because it misstates the modality.

However, looking at the Llama answer: "The Board of Directors of VEREIT or Realty Income, as applicable, may require a new Notice of Recommendation Change and a new Notice Period for any amendment to any material term of a Superior Proposal..."

The canonical claim is identical to the Llama answer. The question asks about "Number of additional matching rights periods for modifications (COR)". The Llama answer captures the mechanism but uses "may" instead of "shall".

Wait, I need to be careful. The prompt says "Use only the contract excerpt, the question, the model answer, and the fixed claim list." So I should evaluate whether the claim is correct based on the contract excerpt.

Contract text: "any amendment to any material term of such Superior Proposal shall require a new Notice of Recommendation Change and a new Notice Period, except that the four (4) Business Day Notice Period referred to in clause (D) above shall instead be equal to the longer of (1) three (3) Business Days or (2) the period remaining under the Notice Period under clause (D) above immediately prior to the delivery of such additional notice under this clause (D)"

Claim text: "The Board of Directors of VEREIT or Realty Income, as applicable, may require a new Notice of Recommendation Change and a new Notice Period for any amendment to any material term of a Superior Proposal, except that the four (4) Business Day Notice Period shall instead be equal to the longer of (1) three (3) Business Days or (2) the period remaining under the Notice Period under clause (D) above immediately prior to the delivery of such additional notice."

Differences:
1. "may require" vs "shall require". The contract says it is mandatory that a new Notice be required. Actually, the contract says "any amendment ... shall require a new Notice...". This means the amendment triggers the requirement. The claim says the Board "may require" which implies discretion. This seems false or partially true.
2. The claim omits "referred to in clause (D) above" but that's minor.
3. The claim omits "under this clause (D)" at the end but that's minor.
4. The claim says "The Board ... may require..." - the contract is a provision about what the parties understand regarding amendments, not necessarily the Board requiring it. Actually, the sentence structure is: "(it being understood that any amendment ... shall require a new Notice ... except that ...)" This is an objective condition, not a discretionary Board action.

So the claim seems to misrepresent the contractual provision by introducing discretion ("may") where there is none ("shall"). However, the Llama answer itself is the basis. Wait, the task says "proxy legal annotator". Usually in these tasks, we compare the claim to the contract and the answer. The claim is identical to the Llama answer. Is the Llama answer correct? The question is "Number of additional matching rights periods for modifications (COR)". The Llama answer doesn't explicitly state a number but describes the mechanism.

But the key issue: is the claim true? The contract says "shall require", not "may require". The claim says "may require". That makes it incorrect in a material way. However, sometimes "may require" could be interpreted as "the circumstances may require" but that's not what it says. It says "The Board ... may require". This is an attribution of discretionary power that doesn't exist in the contract. The contract says the amendment itself shall require a new notice. So the claim is false.

Wait, but let me double-check. Is there any other interpretation? The contract says the Board may make a Change in Recommendation if and only if several conditions are met, including (D) and the understanding about amendments. The parenthetical says "it being understood that any amendment to any material term of such Superior Proposal shall require a new Notice of Recommendation Change and a new Notice Period". This is not the Board exercising discretion; it's a condition that automatically applies. So the claim's "may require" is wrong.

What about load-bearing? A load-bearing claim is one where if you flip the evidence (i.e., negate it or find contradictory evidence), it would change the answer. Or is it a claim that is central to the answer? In these annotation tasks, "load_bearing" usually means whether the claim is a critical, central claim that the answer relies upon. If the claim is false, does it undermine the answer? The answer is essentially just this claim. So yes, it's load-bearing because it's the main substance of the answer.

Wait, looking at the prompt: "load_bearing_label: yes or no". In legal claim annotation tasks (like those from Atticus or similar), "load-bearing" means that the claim is central to the analysis. If the claim is the only claim and constitutes the entire answer, it is definitely load-bearing. Also, if it's false, the answer is wrong.

flip_evidence_text: required if load_bearing_label=yes. This should be the text from the contract that contradicts or supports the flip. Since I labeled it false because of "shall" vs "may", the flip evidence would be the contract text stating "shall require" rather than "may require".

Let me construct the flip_evidence_text. It should be the relevant excerpt from the contract. The contract says: "any amendment to any material term of such Superior Proposal shall require a new Notice of Recommendation Change and a new Notice Period, except that the four (4) Business Day Notice Period referred to in clause (D) above shall instead be equal to the longer of (1) three (3) Business Days or (2) the period remaining under the Notice Period under clause (D) above immediately prior to the delivery of such additional notice under this clause (D)"

So the flip evidence should highlight that it's "shall require" not "may require". I can include the relevant snippet.

Let me reconsider correctness. Is it possible that the claim is "partially_true"? The claim captures most of the mechanism but gets the modality wrong. In many annotation guidelines, a material misstatement like shall vs may makes it false. However, if the rest of the content is correct, some might say partially_true. But "may" vs "shall" is a critical legal distinction. I think false is appropriate. But let's think: is the claim summarizing that the Board may (under the agreement) require a new notice period? Actually, the agreement imposes the requirement. So saying the Board "may require" suggests the Board has a choice, which is not supported. I will label it false.

Wait, actually, looking at the exact wording of the claim again: "The Board of Directors of VEREIT or Realty Income, as applicable, may require a new Notice..." The contract says "any amendment ... shall require a new Notice...". The claim could be read as "The Board ... may [be required to] ..."? No, "may require" clearly means the Board has the option to require. That's not what the contract says.

So:
- correctness_label: false
- load_bearing_label: yes (it's the only claim and the core of the answer)
- flip_evidence_text: The contract states that "any amendment to any material term of such Superior Proposal shall require a new Notice of Recommendation Change and a new Notice Period," not that the Board "may require" such notice.
- notes: Material misstatement of modality; contract mandates ("shall require") rather than permits ("may require") a new notice.

Wait, I should double check if there's any other reading. Could "may require" refer to the Board's power to require a new notice under the agreement? No, the parenthetical is not about Board power; it's about the effect of an amendment. The Board is the subject of the main sentence ("the Board ... may make a Change ... if and only if ..."), and the parenthetical is an understanding about the notice period. So the claim is definitely misattributing the requirement.

Let me also consider if the claim is true. Some annotators might interpret the claim as simply saying "the agreement provides that a new notice may be required"? No, that's not what it says. It explicitly says "The Board ... may require". That's a statement of fact about the Board's authority.

Another thought: Is the question asking for a number? "Number of additional matching rights periods for modifications (COR)". The Llama answer doesn't give a number but describes a mechanism. The claim also doesn't give a number. Is the claim responsive to the question? The question might expect something like "one additional matching rights period" or "three business days". But the claim describes the mechanism. However, my job is not to evaluate responsiveness to the question directly but correctness of the claim relative to the contract. The claim is a statement of what the contract says.

Actually, looking at similar tasks, sometimes the "COR" means "corrected" or it's a specific label. But I don't need to infer that. I just evaluate the claim.

One more check: The prompt says "Use only the contract excerpt, the question, the model answer, and the fixed claim list." So I should consider the question. The question asks for a number. The claim doesn't state a number but states a rule. Does the rule imply the number of additional matching rights periods? It mentions that the notice period shall be equal to the longer of 3 days or the remaining period. This implies there can be multiple additional notice periods (one for each amendment), but the question asks "Number of additional matching rights periods for modifications". The contract doesn't specify a maximum number; it seems each material amendment triggers a new period. So the answer might be "unlimited" or "not specified, but each amendment triggers a new 3+ day period". The claim doesn't answer the number question directly. But as a claim about the contract, is it true? No, because of "may" vs "shall".

Let me reconsider if "may" could be justified. In legal writing, sometimes "may" is used to mean "is permitted to" but here the entity that "shall require" is the amendment itself (or the agreement). The Board is not the subject of "shall require". So saying "The Board ... may require" is introducing a new subject and verb that don't match.

What if the claim is considered partially true because the rest of the quote is accurate? Many annotation rubrics treat minor omissions as partially true, but material changes in meaning as false. "Shall" vs "may" is material. I'll go with false.

Now for the flip_evidence_text. If load_bearing is yes, I need to provide text that flips or supports the flip. The flip here is that the contract uses "shall require" not "may require". So the evidence text is the snippet from the contract.

I need to format the output. The prompt says "For each claim return:". There's only one claim. I'll return a JSON or structured text. Usually these tasks want a JSON-like object. I'll provide it clearly.

Wait, the prompt doesn't specify JSON format explicitly, but says "return:". I should probably return a structured format. Let me see the required fields. I'll output something like:

claim-3d7dfa9bf8de:
- correctness_label: false
- load_bearing_label: yes
- flip_evidence_text: "any amendment to any material term of such Superior Proposal shall require a new Notice of Recommendation Change and a new Notice Period, except that the four (4) Business Day Notice Period referred to in clause (D) above shall instead be equal to the longer of (1) three (3) Business Days or (2) the period remaining under the Notice Period under clause (D) above immediately prior to the delivery of such additional notice under this clause (D)"
- notes: Claim incorrectly states the Board "may require" a new notice; the contract provides that any material amendment "shall require" a new notice, making the requirement mandatory rather than discretionary.

Actually, looking at the exact claim text again: "The Board of Directors of VEREIT or Realty Income, as applicable, may require a new Notice of Recommendation Change and a new Notice Period for any amendment to any material term of a Superior Proposal, except that the four (4) Business Day Notice Period shall instead be equal to the longer of (1) three (3) Business Days or (2) the period remaining under the Notice Period under clause (D) above immediately prior to the delivery of such additional notice."

Wait, the contract says "shall instead be equal to the longer of...". The claim says "shall instead be equal to the longer of...". That part matches. The main issue is "may require". Also, the claim omits "referred to in clause (D) above" and "under this clause (D)" but those are minor.

Let me double-check the contract excerpt for any mention of the Board requiring anything. The contract says: "the Board ... may make a Change ... (and ... terminate ...) ... if and only if ... (D) ... (it being understood that any amendment ... shall require a new Notice ...)".

So the Board is not the one requiring the new notice; it's the amendment that triggers the requirement. The claim is false.

Could the claim be interpreted as "The Board ... may [under the agreement] require..."? Even then, the agreement doesn't say the Board requires it; it says the amendment requires it. It's a subtle but real difference.

I'll stick with false.

Let me think about load-bearing. If this claim is false, the answer is entirely false or at least materially misleading. So it's load-bearing.

One final check: Is there any possibility that the claim is "partially_true"? If I were to be extremely lenient, I might say partially true because it captures the exception mechanism correctly. But the modality is so central to legal obligations that false is better. Also, the question asks for a number, and the claim doesn't provide one but provides a rule. However, the evaluation is about the claim's correctness, not its completeness. The claim makes an assertion about the contract that is incorrect. So false.

Wait, I just noticed: The claim says "may require a new Notice ... for any amendment". Could "may" be functioning as "might" (possibility)? Like "The Board ... may [find it necessary to] require..."? No, that's not a natural reading. In legal annotation tasks, we read claims literally.
